meta.title: Yacht Charter Itineraries — Sailing Routes | Boat4You

indexSeo.h2Choose: How to pick your yacht charter route
indexSeo.introPara1: Boat4You covers six charter playgrounds — Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Türkiye and the Caribbean — and each sails differently: different winds, different distances, different price points. A standard charter week runs marina to marina, Saturday to Saturday, and the sample routes on this page are built around exactly that rhythm.
indexSeo.introPara2: Every route here is bookable in practice: each departure base holds a live fleet on Boat4You, so once a route fits, you can jump straight from the itinerary to the boats actually available from that marina for your dates.
indexSeo.h3Croatia: Croatia — Split, Dubrovnik, Šibenik, Istria & Zadar
indexSeo.croatiaBody: Short hops, sheltered channels and more than a thousand islands make Croatia the easiest place in Europe to sail your first bareboat week. The busiest bases sit around Split and Trogir, with Dubrovnik, Šibenik and Zadar covering the south and north. Expect 10–25 NM legs, an island town every evening and two national parks — Kornati and Mljet — within a week's reach. See boats in Croatia.
indexSeo.greeceBody: Greece splits into four moods: the gentle Ionian for relaxed family weeks, the windy postcard Cyclades for confident crews, the green and quiet Sporades, and the Dodecanese hugging the Turkish coast. Distances stay modest and tavernas replace marinas at many overnight stops. See boats in Greece.
indexSeo.italyBody: Italy is charter for the senses — the Amalfi Coast and Capri from Naples, granite coves and gin-clear water on Sardinia's Costa Smeralda, volcanoes and Aeolian island-hopping from Sicily. The sailing legs are short; the food is the second itinerary. See boats in Italy.
indexSeo.spainBody: The Balearics — Mallorca, Ibiza, Menorca, Formentera — pack cliff-backed anchorages and beach clubs into short daily legs, while Barcelona and the Costa Brava suit crews who want a big-city arrival and a coastal cruise. See boats in Spain.
indexSeo.turkeyBody: The Turquoise Coast runs the longest season in the Mediterranean — April to November — from Bodrum for the Gulf of Gökova or Göcek for Fethiye, Kaş and Kekova's sunken ruins, plus the strongest crewed-gulet fleet anywhere. See boats in Türkiye.
indexSeo.caribbeanBody: Winter charter at its best: steady trade winds, line-of-sight hops in the BVI, shallow turquoise banks in the Bahamas, and the greener, quieter Grenadines and Martinique to the south. The season peaks December to April — exactly when the Mediterranean sleeps. Browse the Caribbean itineraries.
indexSeo.quickFirstTime: First bareboat, or young kids aboard? Croatia from Split or the Greek Ionian — short sheltered legs and forgiving winds.
indexSeo.quickPhotogenic: Chasing the postcard? The Cyclades for white villages over deep blue, Sardinia for Caribbean-clear coves — or the actual Caribbean from Tortola.
indexSeo.quickTwoWeeks: Two weeks to fill? Combine a Split → Dubrovnik one-way, run Athens deep into the Cyclades, or chain Bodrum with Göcek along the Turquoise Coast.
indexSeo.quickFood: Sailing to eat? The Amalfi Coast from Naples, or Mallorca and Menorca — short legs, long dinners.
indexSeo.quickCrewed: Want the crew included? Türkiye's gulets come with captain and chef; larger crewed yachts sail Croatia, Greece and the Caribbean.
indexSeo.stillUnsure: Still deciding? <searchLink>Open the boat search</searchLink> — filter by country, dates and boat type, and the right route usually reveals itself.
